claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility rules vary based on age:
- **Under 16**: Generally qualifies (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: May qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally doesn't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, or if you have multiple children, let me know their ages and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I actually have three children I'd like to ask about - Alex is 7, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for all three of your children: Alex (7), Blake (20), and Charlie (7).

Let me work through each child:

**For Alex (age 7) and Charlie (age 7):**
Since they are both under 16, they fall into the category that generally qualifies for Child Benefit. Let me check the next requirement for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 20):**
Unfortunately, Blake at age 20 is over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit generally only applies to children under 20, and even then, those aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training.

Now, for **Alex and Charlie (both age 7)**, let me continue with the next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

I'm assuming at age 7 they are not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. Can you confirm they are not working?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
That's right, Alex and Charlie aren't working at all - they're only 7! 

And yes, I understand about Blake being 20. He actually works full-time at a local shop, so I figured he probably wouldn't qualify anyway.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! At age 7, Alex and Charlie wouldn't be working. Let me continue with their assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next question for Alex and Charlie:

**Are Alex and Charlie NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by them directly (not you as their parent). 

At age 7, I imagine they're not receiving any benefits in their own right, but I need to confirm - can you verify that neither Alex nor Charlie are claiming ESA or Universal Credit in their own names?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither Alex nor Charlie are receiving any benefits in their own names. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all that sort of thing for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next check for Alex and Charlie:

**Are Alex and Charlie in any type of apprenticeship?**

At age 7, they certainly wouldn't be in an apprenticeship (as these are for older teenagers and adults), but I need to confirm this to move forward. Can you confirm that neither of them is in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - they're only 7! They're in primary school, not doing any apprenticeships.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status as the claimant. This is about **your** situation, not the children's.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common), OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold), OR
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (up to 91 days), OR
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself, OR
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying, OR
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with pre-settled status, OR
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of these other situations apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm not from the EU or anything like that, just a regular UK resident.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! You meet the residency requirement. Now let's check if you're responsible for Alex and Charlie.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

You qualify as responsible if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with them** (most common), OR
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.), OR
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned you're the one handling everything for the family, I'm assuming you live with Alex and Charlie. Can you confirm that you live with them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - both Alex and Charlie live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you'd need to arrange a transfer with them (and they would need to stop their claim).

Are you the only person claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, or is someone else also claiming for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about Alex and Charlie's living situation.

**Are Alex and Charlie currently:**
- In hospital (for an extended stay)?
- In care (with a local authority)?
- Being fostered?
- Living normally at home with you?

Which situation applies to them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex is being fostered - I'm fostering Alex. But Charlie lives normally at home with me, he's my biological child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that! That's important information. Let me assess each child separately since they have different living arrangements.

**For Alex (fostered child):**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (the fostered child):**

This is an important question: **Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you fostering Alex privately (without council payments), or is the council paying you fostering allowances for Alex's care?

- If the council is paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, you generally cannot claim Child Benefit for them
- If the council is NOT paying (private fostering arrangement), you may be able to claim

What's the situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying me anything for Alex. I'm not getting any maintenance or accommodation money from them for fostering Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since the council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, let me proceed to the final check for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me check Charlie's situation. You mentioned Charlie lives normally at home with you as your biological child. Let me continue with Charlie's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (your biological child living at home):**

Just to confirm the final requirement: **Is the local council NOT paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Charlie is your biological child living at home with you normally, I'm assuming the council isn't paying for Charlie's care, but I need to confirm this. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything for Charlie. He's just my child living at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for all three of your children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

Great news! Here's the outcome for your three children:

---

### ✅ **ALEX (Age 7) - ELIGIBLE**
You **CAN** claim Child Benefit for Alex. Even though Alex is fostered, you meet all the requirements because:
- Alex is under 16
- The council is NOT paying you for Alex's accommodation or maintenance
- You live with and are responsible for Alex
- All other eligibility criteria are met

---

### ❌ **BLAKE (Age 20) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
You **CANNOT** claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake is 20 years old, which is over the age limit
- Child Benefit only applies to children under 16, or young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training
- At age 20, Blake doesn't qualify regardless of other circumstances

---

### ✅ **CHARLIE (Age 7) - ELIGIBLE**
You **CAN** claim Child Benefit for Charlie. As your biological child living at home, Charlie meets all the requirements:
- Charlie is under 16
- Lives at home with you normally
- The council is not paying for Charlie's care
- All other eligibility criteria are met

---

## **Next Steps for Alex and Charlie:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates if applicable)
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return
5. **Payments**: You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**You can apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie?

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and you are fostering Alex without any council payments for accommodation or maintenance. You live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the eligibility requirements.

Blake is 20 years old, which is over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. At age 20, Blake does not qualify regardless of other circumstances.

Charlie is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives at home with you as your biological child. The council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. You live in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie meets all the eligibility requirements.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children: Alex (fostered child, age 7) and Charlie (biological child, age 7). Both meet all eligibility criteria. Blake (age 20) does not qualify as he is over the age limit for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and you are fostering Alex without any council payments for accommodation or maintenance. You live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the eligibility requirements.

Blake is 20 years old, which is over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. At age 20, Blake does not qualify regardless of other circumstances.

Charlie is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives at home with you as your biological child. The council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. You live in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie meets all the eligibility requirements.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children: Alex (fostered child, age 7) and Charlie (biological child, age 7). Both meet all eligibility criteria. Blake (age 20) does not qualify as he is over the age limit for Child Benefit.
